section.title: John's Ministry, the Baptism, and the Genealogy
section.sectionRationale: The infancy narratives close and the public story begins. Luke dates John's ministry by emperors and governors, then lets the forerunner preach a baptism of repentance and point beyond himself to the Mightier One. Jesus takes His place with sinners in the water, the Spirit descends, and the Father speaks. Luke then traces the line back, not to Abraham only, but all the way to Adam, the son of God, framing Jesus as the second Adam for the whole human race.
section.thesis: The forerunner calls Israel to a repentance that bears fruit and points to the Coming One; and when Jesus stands with sinners in the Jordan, the Spirit descends and the Father names Him beloved Son, the last Adam who will succeed where the first fell.
section.frameNote: Five movements: John's prophetic call dated in world history; his preaching of repentance and its fruit; his witness to the Mightier One who baptizes with the Spirit and fire; the baptism of Jesus with the descending Spirit and the Father's voice; and the genealogy running back through David to Adam and to God.

units.label: John's prophetic call in history
units.note: the word of God comes in the wilderness

verses.text: In the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar, while Pontius Pilate was governor of Judea, Herod tetrarch of Galilee, his brother Philip tetrarch of Ituraea and Trachonitis, and Lysanias tetrarch of Abilene,
during the high priesthood of Annas and Caiaphas, the word of God came to John son of Zechariah in the wilderness.
He went into all the region around the Jordan, preaching a baptism of {{metanoia|repentance}} for the forgiveness of sins,
as it is written in the book of the words of Isaiah the prophet: “A voice of one calling in the wilderness, ‘Prepare the way for the Lord, make straight paths for Him.
Every valley shall be filled in, and every mountain and hill made low. The crooked ways shall be made straight, and the rough ways smooth.
And all humanity will see God’s salvation.’”

units.label: A baptism of repentance and its fruit
units.note: produce fruit in keeping with repentance

verses.text: Then John said to the crowds coming out to be baptized by him, “You brood of vipers, who warned you to flee from the coming wrath?

Produce fruit, then, in keeping with {{metanoia|repentance}}. And do not begin to say to yourselves, ‘We have Abraham as our father.’ For I tell you that out of these stones God can raise up children for Abraham.
The axe lies ready at the root of the trees, and every tree that does not produce good fruit will be cut down and thrown into the fire.”
The crowds asked him, “What then should we do?”
John replied, “Whoever has two tunics should share with him who has none, and whoever has food should do the same.”
Even tax collectors came to be baptized. “Teacher,” they asked, “what should we do?”
“Collect no more than you are authorized,” he answered.
Then some soldiers asked him, “And what should we do?” “Do not take money by force or false accusation,” he said. “Be content with your wages.”

units.label: The One more powerful is coming
units.note: he will baptize with the Spirit and fire

verses.text: The people were waiting expectantly and were all wondering in their hearts if John could be the {{christos|Christ}}.

John answered all of them: “I {{baptizo|baptize}} you with water, but One more powerful than I will come, the straps of whose sandals I am not worthy to untie. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and with fire.
His winnowing fork is in His hand to clear His threshing floor and to gather the wheat into His barn; but He will burn up the chaff with unquenchable fire.”
With these and many other exhortations, John {{euangelizo|proclaimed the good news}} to the people.
But when he rebuked Herod the tetrarch regarding his brother’s wife Herodias and all the evils he had done,
Herod added this to them all: He locked John up in prison.
When all the people were being baptized, Jesus was baptized too. And as He was praying, heaven was opened,
and the Holy Spirit descended on Him in a bodily form like a dove. And a voice came from heaven: “You are My beloved Son; in You I am well pleased.”

units.label: The genealogy to Adam and to God
units.note: son of Adam, son of God

verses.text: Jesus Himself was about thirty years old when He began His ministry. He was regarded as the son of Joseph, the son of Heli,
the son of Matthat, the son of Levi, the son of Melchi, the son of Jannai, the son of Joseph,
the son of Mattathias, the son of Amos, the son of Nahum, the son of Esli, the son of Naggai,
the son of Maath, the son of Mattathias, the son of Semein, the son of Josech, the son of Joda,
the son of Joanan, the son of Rhesa, the son of Zerubbabel, the son of Shealtiel, the son of Neri,
the son of Melchi, the son of Addi, the son of Cosam, the son of Elmadam, the son of Er,
the son of Joshua, the son of Eliezer, the son of Jorim, the son of Matthat, the son of Levi,
the son of Simeon, the son of Judah, the son of Joseph, the son of Jonam, the son of Eliakim,
the son of Melea, the son of Menna, the son of Mattatha, the son of Nathan, the son of David,
the son of Jesse, the son of Obed, the son of Boaz, the son of Sala, the son of Nahshon,
the son of Amminadab, the son of Admin, the son of Arni, the son of Hezron, the son of Perez, the son of Judah,
the son of Jacob, the son of Isaac, the son of Abraham, the son of Terah, the son of Nahor,
the son of Serug, the son of Reu, the son of Peleg, the son of Eber, the son of Shelah,
the son of Cainan, the son of Arphaxad, the son of Shem, the son of Noah, the son of Lamech,
the son of Methuselah, the son of Enoch, the son of Jared, the son of Mahalalel, the son of Cainan,
the son of Enosh, the son of Seth, the son of Adam, the son of God.

metanoia.gloss: A change of mind that turns the whole life around; repentance. It is not mere regret but a reorientation away from sin toward God. John preaches a baptism of metanoia and insists it must show itself in fruit, in real changes of behavior toward the poor, in honest work, in contentment.

baptizo.gloss: To dip, immerse, or wash; to baptize. John baptizes with water as a sign of repentance, but he points to One who will baptize with the Holy Spirit and with fire, an immersion in the very presence and purifying power of God that only the Messiah can give.

christos.gloss: The Anointed One, the Messiah, the Greek equivalent of the Hebrew Mashiach. The crowds wonder whether John himself might be the Christ; John firmly points away from himself to the Mightier One whose sandals he is unworthy to untie.

euangelizo.gloss: To announce good news, to proclaim glad tidings. Luke says that even John's stern call to repentance was gospel: it prepared the way for the salvation all humanity would see, so that the warning of judgment and the offer of mercy belong to one good announcement.

basic.context: After the birth stories, Luke opens the public history. He carefully dates John's ministry by the rulers of the day, then lets John preach in the wilderness. John calls the crowds to repent and to show it by changed lives, warns that heritage alone will not save them, and points beyond himself to a Mightier One. When Jesus is baptized, the Spirit comes down and the Father speaks. Luke then lists Jesus' ancestry all the way back to Adam and to God.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the dating in verses 1 and 2 as Luke's way of anchoring the gospel in real history: this happened under named emperors, governors, and priests. John's preaching is descriptive of his mission but also lays down an abiding pattern, real repentance bears fruit. The baptism scene is the hinge, where the Father, Son, and Spirit are all present. The genealogy is not filler; its final line, son of Adam, son of God, tells us who Jesus is.
basic.originalLanguages: John preaches metanoia, a repentance that turns the whole life around and shows in fruit. He baptizes with water, but points to One who will baptizo, immerse, people in the Holy Spirit and fire. The crowds wonder if John is the christos, the Anointed One; he points away from himself. And Luke calls even John's hard words euangelizo, good news, because they prepare the way for the salvation all will see.
basic.typology: John is the promised voice in the wilderness of Isaiah 40, preparing the Lord's way. The descending dove recalls the Spirit hovering over the waters at creation and the dove of Noah's new beginning. Above all, the genealogy back to Adam presents Jesus as the second Adam, the true Son who will stand where the first son of God fell.
basic.christ: Jesus, though sinless, steps into the water with sinners, identifying with the people He came to save. The Spirit rests on Him and the Father names Him beloved Son, echoing Psalm 2 and Isaiah 42, marking Him as King and Servant. As the son of Adam and Son of God, He is the new head of a renewed humanity.
basic.god: God speaks His word to an ordinary man in a desert, keeps His promise to send a forerunner, and reveals Himself as Father, Son, and Spirit at the Jordan. He is a God who judges fruitless trees yet offers a baptism of repentance and forgiveness, a God whose salvation all humanity will see.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 40:3-5 is the prophecy John fulfills. Psalm 2:7 and Isaiah 42:1 stand behind the Father's words. Acts 1:5 and 2:1-4 show the promised baptism with the Spirit poured out. Romans 5:12-19 and 1 Corinthians 15:45 unfold Jesus as the last Adam.
basic.oneStory: The long wait is ending. The forerunner prepares the way, the King steps into the water with sinners, and the Spirit and the Father mark Him out. By tracing the line to Adam, Luke shows that this is not merely Israel's story but the whole world's: the second Adam has come to undo the fall and to bring the salvation all humanity will see.

context.literary: This section turns Luke from the infancy narratives to the public ministry. It moves in ordered scenes: a historical dating, John's preaching with sample questions and answers, John's witness to the Coming One, the baptism of Jesus, and a genealogy that closes the movement by reaching back to Adam. The Isaiah citation frames John, and the Father's voice frames Jesus.
context.historical: The reign of Tiberius, Pilate's governorship, the Herodian tetrarchs, and the high priesthood of Annas and Caiaphas fix the events around AD 28 to 29. John's wilderness ministry and baptism drew crowds, tax collectors, and soldiers. Herod Antipas would later imprison and behead John for rebuking his marriage to Herodias.
context.canonical: John fulfills the promise of a preparing voice, Isaiah 40 and Malachi 3. The baptism reveals the Trinity and launches the ministry the whole Gospel unfolds. Luke's genealogy, unlike Matthew's descent from Abraham, ascends to Adam and God, tying Jesus to all humanity and setting up the second Adam theology developed by Paul.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To root the gospel in datable history, to present John as the promised forerunner calling for real repentance, to reveal Jesus at His baptism as the Spirit-anointed, beloved Son, and through the genealogy to present Him as the second Adam and Savior of all humanity.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The historical dating and John's specific counsel to crowds, tax collectors, and soldiers describe his particular ministry. The enduring principle is that genuine repentance always bears visible fruit and that no one is saved by heritage. The baptism and the Father's declaration are revelation about who Jesus is, not a pattern we reenact in the same way.

debates.question: Why was the sinless Jesus baptized with a baptism of repentance?

views.label: To identify with sinners
views.case: Jesus had no sin to repent of, but He steps into the water alongside the people He came to save, taking His place with them and beginning the path that leads to the cross where He bears their sin.

views.label: To fulfill all righteousness and inaugurate His mission
views.case: As Matthew 3:15 puts it, His baptism fulfills all righteousness; it is His public anointing by the Spirit and commissioning as the obedient Son, the start of His public ministry.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree Jesus is not confessing personal sin; His baptism is an act of solidarity and obedience that reveals Him as the Spirit-anointed Son who saves sinners.

debates.question: Why does Luke's genealogy differ from Matthew's and run back to Adam?

views.label: Different purpose and possibly different line
views.case: Matthew descends from Abraham through the royal line to show Jesus as Israel's King and covenant heir; Luke ascends to Adam to show Jesus as Savior of all humanity, and many hold the two lines trace different paths through Joseph and possibly Mary.

views.label: A theological climax in Adam and God
views.case: Whatever the exact links, Luke's aim is clear in the last line, son of Adam, son of God, presenting Jesus as the second Adam who succeeds where the first failed.

debates.bearing: The point is not to resolve every name but to see Jesus as the true Son of God and the new head of the human race, reaching beyond Israel to the world.

study.originalLanguages: John's word is metanoia, a repentance that turns the whole life around and must bear fruit; heritage from Abraham cannot substitute for it. He baptizes with water, but the Coming One will baptizo, immerse, people in the Holy Spirit and fire, a purifying and empowering that only the Messiah gives. The crowds ask whether John is the christos, the Anointed One, and he points away from himself to the Mightier One. Luke even calls John's severe preaching euangelizo, good news, because it prepares the way for the salvation all humanity will see. At the Jordan the Spirit descends and the Father names Jesus beloved Son, and the genealogy closes with son of Adam, son of God.

typology.type: The voice in the wilderness, Isaiah 40
typology.fulfillment: John the Baptist, who prepares the way of the Lord and levels the road for the coming salvation.

typology.type: The Spirit descending like a dove
typology.fulfillment: The Spirit who hovered over creation's waters and rested after the flood now rests on Jesus, marking the dawn of new creation.

typology.type: Adam, the first son of God
typology.fulfillment: Jesus the second Adam, the obedient Son who stands where the first fell and heads a renewed humanity, 1 Corinthians 15:45 and Romans 5:19.

study.christ: Jesus is revealed here as the Spirit-anointed, beloved Son. Though without sin, He is baptized with sinners, taking His place among the people He came to redeem and beginning the road to the cross. The Father's words weave together Psalm 2:7, the enthroned King, and Isaiah 42:1, the chosen Servant, so that Jesus is King and suffering Servant in one. The genealogy names Him son of Adam and Son of God, the new Adam who will succeed where the first failed and bring salvation to all humanity, not to Israel only.
study.god: The living God still speaks: His word comes to John in the desert, and His voice sounds from heaven over His Son. He keeps every promise, sending the forerunner and revealing the salvation foretold by Isaiah. At the Jordan He shows Himself as Father, Son, and Spirit together. He is holy, warning of the axe at the root and the unquenchable fire, yet full of grace, offering a baptism of repentance and forgiveness, and declaring His delight in His beloved Son.

crossReferences.note: The voice preparing the Lord's way, quoted by Luke and fulfilled in John's wilderness ministry.
You are My Son; the royal enthronement psalm echoed in the Father's declaration at the baptism.
My chosen One in whom I delight, on whom I put My Spirit, standing behind the words over Jesus.
Jesus as the last Adam, the life-giving Spirit, contrasted with the first man, the point of Luke's genealogy back to Adam.

study.oneStory: The forerunner promised by the prophets steps onto the stage of datable history and prepares the Lord's way, calling for a repentance that bears fruit. Then the King Himself stands in the water with sinners, the Spirit descends, and the Father names Him beloved Son. By tracing the line past Abraham to Adam and to God, Luke tells us this is the world's story: the second Adam has come to undo the fall and to bring the salvation all humanity will see, a salvation the rest of the Gospel unfolds.

sermon.title: The Baptism of Jesus
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Luke, this message on Luke 3:21 explains why the sinless Jesus was baptized, how His baptism identified Him with the sinners He came to save, and how it inaugurated His public ministry as the Spirit-anointed, beloved Son. A faithful, expository handling of the climax of this movement.

quiz.q: What did John call the crowds to do?

options.text: To repent and produce fruit in keeping with repentance, not resting on their descent from Abraham.
options.note: This is exactly John's message: a baptism of repentance that shows itself in changed lives, verses 8 to 14.

options.text: To be baptized as an outward sign of turning to God.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but John insists the sign must be matched by real fruit, so this is only part of the picture.

options.text: To claim Abraham as their father and so be safe from wrath.
options.note: This is the very presumption John warns against; heritage without repentance cannot save.

options.text: To take up arms against Rome and Herod.
options.note: John never calls for revolt; he tells even soldiers to be content and just.

quiz.q: How did John describe the One who was coming after him?

options.text: As One more powerful, whose sandals John was unworthy to untie, who would baptize with the Holy Spirit and with fire.
options.note: This is John's own testimony in verse 16, pointing away from himself to the Mightier One.

options.text: As a great prophet in the line of Elijah.
options.note: The Coming One is greater than any prophet; this understates who He is, though it moves in the right direction.

options.text: As a political liberator who would overthrow Herod.
options.note: A misplaced, worldly expectation; John speaks of a baptism with the Spirit and fire, not a coup.

options.text: As no one greater than himself, since John was the Christ.
options.note: John flatly denies being the Christ and points to the One mightier than he.

quiz.q: What happened when Jesus was baptized?

options.text: Heaven opened, the Spirit descended like a dove, and the Father's voice named Him His beloved Son.
options.note: Verses 21 and 22 reveal the Father, Son, and Spirit together at the Jordan.

options.text: Jesus identified with sinners by taking His place in the water.
options.note: True and important, but it names the meaning of His act rather than the full event Luke records.

options.text: It was an ordinary baptism with nothing to set it apart.
options.note: This misses the descending Spirit and the Father's voice that mark this baptism as unique.

options.text: Jesus was baptized to be cleansed from His own sin.
options.note: Jesus is sinless; His baptism is solidarity and obedience, not personal cleansing.

quiz.q: Why does Luke trace Jesus' genealogy back to Adam, the son of God?

options.text: To present Jesus as the second Adam and Savior of all humanity, not of Israel only.
options.note: The closing line, son of Adam, son of God, frames Jesus as the new head of the human race.

options.text: To establish Jesus' legal descent through Joseph.
options.note: Luke does trace the line through Joseph, but the deeper aim is the reach back to Adam and God.

options.text: To show Jesus was descended only from David.
options.note: David is in the list, but Luke goes far beyond him to Adam; limiting it to David misses the point.

options.text: To suggest Jesus was just one more ordinary link in a human chain.
options.note: The opposite: the genealogy exalts Jesus as the Son of God and the new Adam.

quiz.q: What does John's image of the axe at the root of the trees teach?

options.text: That God looks for real fruit, and repentance that produces none faces judgment.
options.note: Verse 9 warns that every fruitless tree is cut down; repentance must be genuine.

options.text: That doing enough good works will earn God's acceptance.
options.note: Fruit matters, but it flows from true repentance rather than earning God's favor; this half-truth misplaces the emphasis.

options.text: That religious heritage will shelter us from judgment.
options.note: This is precisely what John denies when he says God can raise up children for Abraham from stones.

options.text: That God never actually judges anyone.
options.note: The image is a clear warning of real judgment on fruitlessness.

questions: John tied real repentance to real fruit, sharing clothes and food, honest work, contentment. Where might genuine repentance be asking for visible change in your everyday life?
The crowds trusted their descent from Abraham. What heritage, reputation, or past experience are you tempted to rest in instead of ongoing repentance and faith?
Jesus, though sinless, stepped into the water alongside sinners. How does it move you that your Savior was willing to stand in your place from the very start?
At the Jordan the Father said, 'You are My beloved Son; in You I am well pleased.' How would it change your day to live as one loved by God in His beloved Son?
Luke calls even John's hard words good news, because they prepared the way for salvation. Where is God using a hard word to prepare a way of grace in your life right now?

translationNotes.note: All humanity will see God's salvation renders literally all flesh will see the salvation of God, quoting Isaiah 40:3-5 in the Greek Septuagint. Luke extends the Isaiah citation further than Matthew or Mark to stress that salvation reaches beyond Israel to all people.

Baptize you with water and with the Holy Spirit and with fire can also be rendered in water and in the Holy Spirit and in fire, as the BSB footnote notes. The fire suggests both purifying and judgment, gathered up in the winnowing image of verse 17.

You are My beloved Son; in You I am well pleased draws on Psalm 2:7 and Isaiah 42:1. A few Western witnesses, such as Codex Bezae, read the fuller words of Psalm 2:7, You are My Son, today I have begotten You; the BSB follows the widely attested reading.

Luke's genealogy ascends from Joseph to Adam and God, differing from Matthew's descending list from Abraham. Verse 23 says Jesus was regarded as the son of Joseph, guarding the virgin birth already told in chapters 1 and 2, and many take Luke's line to trace Jesus' ancestry in a distinct way from Matthew's royal line.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 3:1-38. Headings, line breaks, poem and note markers, and footnotes were skipped when assembling verse text. No em or en dashes appear in this chapter; none needed conversion.
